sections.heading: Immunity of  the  administrator,  conservator  and  special administrator
sections.title: Immunity for administrators and conservators
sections.summary: The administrator, conservator, and special administrator are immune from civil or criminal proceedings for acts performed in good faith during their appointment. This immunity applies to all actions taken in the course of their duties under the appointment.

requirements.text: The administrator, conservator, and special administrator are immune from civil or criminal proceedings for acts performed in good faith during their appointment

sections.source_quote: The administrator, conservator and special administrator or any person acting on their behalf shall not be liable to any proceedings, whether civil or criminal, for, or on account of, or in respect of, an act done or statement made, or omitted to be done or made, in the bona fide performance of a function under his appointment.
sections.relevance_reason: Immunity for administrators not relevant to data compliance

sections.heading: Interpretation
sections.title: Definitions of key terms in insurance law
sections.summary: This section provides detailed definitions for terms used throughout the Insurance Act. It specifies meanings for terms like 'insurer', 'insurance business', 'general insurance business', 'long term insurance business', 'policyholder', and many others. The definitions are crucial for understanding the scope and application of the Act, particularly in distinguishing between different types of insurance activities and entities.

Advertisement includes all forms of advertising across media
Affiliated company means a parent or subsidiary company or related entities
Assigned capital for foreign companies means funds transferred from abroad
Associate has the meaning assigned by the Second Schedule
Auditor means a person qualified under Financial Reporting Act 2004 and appointed with Commission approval
Authorised agent has the same meaning as in Companies Act
Balance sheet date has the same meaning as in Companies Act
Bank has the same meaning as in Banking Act 2
Captive insurance business means a category specified in Captive Insurance Act 2015
Category means general insurance business and long term insurance business
Chief Executive has the same meaning as in Financial Services Act 2007

Compensation Fund means Insurance Industry Compensation Fund established under section 88
Conservator means a conservator appointed under section 106
Controller has the same meaning as in Financial Services Act 2007
Corporation has the same meaning as in Financial Services Act 2007
Court means Bankruptcy Division of Supreme Court
Derivative has the same meaning as in Securities Act 2005
Designated fund means a fund established by long term insurer for a class of long term insurance business
Director has the meaning assigned in Companies Act 2001
Enforcement Committee means Enforcement Committee established under Financial Services Act 2007
Established surplus and deficit means amounts shown by actuarial investigation
External insurance business means insurance business restricted to non-Mauritian policies
External insurer means a corporation licensed to carry on external insurance business

Insurance broker means a person arranging insurance business with insurers
Insurance business means business of undertaking liability by way of insurance or reinsurance
Insurance manager means a person managing insurance business originating from outside Mauritius
Insurance policy includes a contract of insurance to provide policy benefits

sections.heading: Administration of Act
sections.title: Existing insurance intermediaries deemed licensed
sections.summary: Existing insurance intermediaries and managers are deemed licensed or registered under the new act until January 1, 2008, unless their license or registration is revoked earlier. This ensures continuity of intermediaries' regulatory status during the transition period.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures existing insurance intermediaries maintain regulatory status during transition, which impacts data handling and transfer processes for intermediary data.

requirements.text: An existing insurance intermediary or manager shall be deemed licensed or registered under this Act as an insurance manager, agent, broker or salesperson

sections.source_quote: An existing insurance intermediary or manager shall, until 1 January 2008, be deemed to be licensed or registered under this Act as an insurance manager, agent, broker or salesperson, as the case may be unless the licence or registration is earlier cancelled or revoked under this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Deemed licensing affects financial data handling

sections.heading: Classification of insurance business
sections.title: Existing registers deemed established under new act
sections.summary: Registers maintained by the Commission for existing insurers, managers and intermediaries are deemed to be established and maintained under section 6 of the new act. This provides continuity for regulatory records during the transition period.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures regulatory records for existing entities remain valid under the new act, which affects data storage and transfer compliance for insurance-related data.

requirements.text: Any register held by the Commission relating to existing insurers, insurance managers and intermediaries shall be deemed established and maintained under section 6 of this Act

sections.source_quote: Any register held by the Commission relating to existing insurers, insurance managers and intermediaries shall be deemed to have been established and maintained under section 6 of this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory continuity for financial data registers

sections.heading: Application of Act
sections.title: Application of the Insurance Act
sections.summary: This section specifies when the Act applies and exceptions. It states that the Act does not affect the Sugar Insurance Fund, and it does not apply to captive insurance business except where regulations under the Captive Insurance Act 2015 provide otherwise. The Minister may exclude certain entities or activities from the Act's application, restrict insurance of risks related to assets in Mauritius, or declare certain services as not constituting insurance business.

requirements.text: Nothing in this Act affects the status and operation of Sugar Insurance Fund
This Act does not apply to captive insurance business except where regulations under Captive Insurance Act 2015 provide otherwise
Minister may, on Commission recommendation, by regulations exclude certain entities or activities from application of this Act
Minister may by regulations restrict insurance of risks related to assets in Mauritius with insurers not licensed under this Act
Minister may declare certain services and activities ancillary to insurance business as not constituting insurance business
Commission determines matters of doubt regarding whether an activity constitutes insurance business to which this Act applies

sections.source_quote: 5. Application of Act (1) Nothing in this Act shall affect the status and the operation of the Sugar Insurance Fund set up under the Sugar Insurance Fund Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Minister's regulatory powers over insurance scope

sections.heading: Registers
sections.title: Commission register for insurance licensees and registered persons
sections.summary: The Commission must maintain a register of licensees and registered persons with details in an appropriate format. Licensees and registered persons can inspect the register during normal hours and obtain certified copies of entries upon paying a fee. Copies of register entries are admissible as prima facie evidence in legal proceedings. The Commission may update the register for accuracy and publish updated extracts on its website.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires a public register for insurance licensees, which may impact data storage and transparency in cross-border insurance operations.

requirements.text: The Commission shall establish and maintain a register containing particulars and details of licensees and registered persons
A person may inspect the register during normal office hours and take copies of any entry upon payment of a prescribed fee
A person may obtain a copy certified by the Commission as correct of any entry in or extract of the register upon payment of a prescribed fee
A copy of any entry in the register or extract given under subsection (2) shall be received in any proceedings as prima facie evidence of the entry or extract
The Commission may make additions, erasures or other alterations to the registers to ensure accuracy
The Commission may post on its website parts or extracts of the register updated at regular intervals

sections.source_quote: 6. The Commission shall establish and maintain a register containing such particulars and details of licensees and registered persons, and in such form, manner and medium as it thinks appropriate.
sections.relevance_reason: Record keeping for licensees

sections.heading: General restrictions on insurance business
sections.title: Prohibition on insurance business without Commission licence
sections.summary: No person may carry out insurance business in or from within Mauritius without a Commission licence. Corporations cannot conduct insurance business outside Mauritius without Commission approval. The definition of 'carrying on insurance business' includes activities like underwriting, premium collection, claims handling, and advertising. Contravention results in fines up to 2 million rupees and imprisonment up to 8 years.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts cross-border insurance operations requiring Commission approval for foreign insurance activities, impacting data transfer and international operations.

requirements.text: No person shall carry on, or hold himself out as carrying on, insurance business of any category or class in or from within Mauritius except under the authority of a licence issued by the Commission under section 11

A corporation set up and organised under the laws of Mauritius shall not without the approval of the Commission carry on, or hold itself out as carrying on, insurance business of any class in or from outside Mauritius
A person shall be deemed to carry on insurance business in or from within Mauritius where that person performs any act in Mauritius that results in another person entering into an insurance contract

A person shall be deemed to carry on insurance business in or from within Mauritius where that person occupies premises in Mauritius or makes it known by advertisement that they may be contacted at a particular address in Mauritius
A person shall be deemed to carry on insurance business in or from within Mauritius where they invite a person in Mauritius to enter into or offer to enter into an insurance contract

A person shall be deemed to carry on insurance business in or from within Mauritius where they conduct themselves in a way that reasonably infers they intend to carry on insurance business in or from within Mauritius

An advertisement issued or other invitation made, or an act performed by any person on behalf of or to the order of another person shall be construed as an advertisement or invitation made by that other person
Any person who contravenes this section shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine not exceeding 2 million rupees and imprisonment for a term not exceeding 8 years

sections.source_quote: 7. No person shall carry on, or hold himself out as carrying on, insurance business of any category or class, in or from within Mauritius except under the authority of a licence issued by the Commission under section 11 in respect of that category or class of insurance business.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers and penalties for cross-border insurance business

sections.heading: Restriction on composite insurance business
sections.title: Restrictions on composite insurance business operations
sections.summary: No insurer other than a professional reinsurer may carry out both long-term and general insurance business. The Commission will not grant licences for long-term insurance to insurers already engaged in general insurance or vice versa. Exceptions allow carrying out both types if one is restricted to reinsurance or if one class is incidental to the other in terms of premium income. Insurers must manage separate operations if authorized.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts cross-border insurance operations by requiring separation of long-term and general insurance business, impacting data storage and processing.

requirements.text: No insurer other than a professional reinsurer shall carry on both long term and general insurance business
The Commission shall not grant a licence in respect of long term insurance business to an insurer already engaging in general insurance business
The Commission shall not grant a licence in respect of general insurance business to an insurer already engaging in long term insurance business
The Commission may authorise an insurer to carry on both long term insurance business and general insurance business where either is restricted exclusively to reinsurance

The Commission may authorise an insurer to carry on both long term insurance business and general insurance business where one class of insurance business is or will be incidental in terms of premium income to the principal insurance business
Where authorisation is granted under subsection (3), the insurer shall manage its long term insurance and general insurance business separately in such manner as may be determined by the Commission

sections.source_quote: 8. Subject to the other provisions of this section, no insurer other than a professional reinsurer shall carry on both long term and general insurance business.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: insurance business separation not directly data compliance

sections.heading: Reinsurance
sections.title: Reinsurance licensing conditions
sections.summary: Insurers licensed under this Act must only engage in reinsurance business if they are professional reinsurers or explicitly authorized by the Commission. Professional reinsurers may carry out reinsurance without restrictions, but foreign reinsurers operating in Mauritius are exempt from local licensing requirements. The Commission can set conditions for risks ceded from Mauritian insurers to reinsurers and specify eligible reinsurers. Insurers must cede a prescribed percentage of risks to statutory reinsurers, and the Commission may exempt insurers from this requirement if necessary. The Minister may issue regulations for professional reinsurers.
sections.dtia_summary: Reinsurance activities involving Mauritian insurers must comply with specific licensing conditions for foreign reinsurers, with the Commission setting rules for risk cession and eligible reinsurers. This affects cross-border reinsurance data flows and requires assessment of reinsurance arrangements.

requirements.text: An insurer licensed under this Part shall not underwrite or reinsure risks from another insurer unless licensed as a professional reinsurer or expressly authorized by the Commission
A professional reinsurer licensed under section 11 may carry on reinsurance business of any category or class except where specifically restricted
Risks situated in Mauritius may be ceded to a reinsurer under conditions specified by the Commission
The Commission may specify classes of reinsurers to whom risks underwritten by insurers in Mauritius may be ceded
An insurer shall offer to cede to a statutory reinsurer such percentage of its relevant risks as may be prescribed
The Commission may exempt any insurer from compliance with subsection (5) if it is necessary or appropriate in the circumstances
The Minister may make regulations for professional reinsurers

sections.source_quote: An insurer licensed under this Part shall not underwrite or reinsure risks from another insurer or otherwise carry on reinsurance business except where – (a) it is licensed as a professional reinsurer to carry on solely reinsurance business; or (b) it is otherwise authorised expressly by the Commission on such conditions as it may deem fit.
sections.relevance_reason: Reinsurance licensing conditions for cross-border data transfers

sections.heading: Application for a licence as insurer
sections.title: Insurer license application requirements
sections.summary: Applicants for insurance licenses must submit detailed business plans, shareholder information, feasibility studies certified by actuaries, and other documents as required by the Commission. The Commission may request additional information, verify submissions, and determine applications lapse if requirements are not met. Applicants must notify the Commission of material changes after licensing. False statements or reckless misrepresentations can lead to fines up to 500,000 rupees and imprisonment for up to two years.
sections.dtia_summary: License applications require detailed data collection and verification of applicant information, impacting data sharing and compliance with insurance business operations. This affects data subject rights and regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: An application for a licence under this Part shall be made in such form, manner and medium as the Commission may determine
An application shall be accompanied by a statement of proposed insurance business, including details on the class of insurance business
An application shall be accompanied by particulars of any substantial shareholder or person proposed to act as officer
An application shall be accompanied by a business plan together with a feasibility study conducted and certified by an actuary
An application shall be accompanied by such other information and documents as may be required by the Commission
An application shall be accompanied by the prescribed fee
The Commission may require the applicant or any person referred to in subsection (1)(b) to provide additional information or documents
The Commission may require that any information furnished by the applicant be verified in such manner as it may direct
Where an applicant fails to comply with a requirement made under subsection (2) or (3), the Commission may determine that the application shall lapse
The applicant shall notify the Commission of any material change which may have occurred in the information or documents submitted

Any person who makes a false representation or recklessly makes a false statement in a material particular shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine not exceeding 500,000 rupees and imprisonment for up to 2 years

sections.source_quote: An application for a licence under this Part shall be made in such form, manner and medium as the Commission may determine and shall be accompanied by – (a) a statement of the applicant's proposed insurance business, including details on the class of insurance business to be carried on; (b) particulars of any substantial shareholder, or of any person being proposed to act as officer of the proposed insurer; (c) a business plan together with a feasibility study conducted certified by an actuary; (d) such other information and documents as may be required by the Commission; and (e) the prescribed fee.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers and financial data requirements

sections.heading: Grant of a licence
sections.title: Insurance license grant conditions
sections.summary: The Commission grants insurance licenses only if the applicant meets specific criteria including limited business scope, disclosure of relevant information, financial resources, fit and proper management, compliance with the Act, foreign company experience requirements, public interest considerations, and economic benefits to Mauritius. The Commission may consider international standards, regulations, and foreign regulator information when evaluating applications.
sections.dtia_summary: License grants require assessment of data storage and processing practices across borders, with specific conditions for foreign insurers operating in Mauritius. This impacts data residency and cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: Where the Commission is satisfied that an application under section 10 ought to be granted, it shall grant the application and issue a licence authorising the applicant to carry on such category of insurance business
A licence issued under subsection (1) shall authorise the carrying on of insurance business in or from within Mauritius or in or from within a country outside Mauritius
The Commission shall not grant an application unless the applicant's objects are limited to insurance business and operations arising directly from it
The Commission shall not grant an application unless the applicant has disclosed such bona fide information as the Commission has requested
The Commission shall not grant an application unless the applicant has the financial resources, organisation and management capacities necessary to carry on the business
The Commission shall not grant an application unless the applicant, substantial shareholders, and officers are fit and proper persons to ensure sound and prudent management
The Commission shall not grant an application unless the applicant will be able to comply with and fulfill all requirements under this Act

The Commission shall not grant an application for a foreign company unless it is registered under the Companies Act and has a satisfactory record of at least 3 years experience in handling the category and class of insurance business
The Commission shall not grant an application unless the interests of the public and policyholders will not be jeopardised by the grant of a licence
The Commission shall not grant an application unless it would be in the economic interests of Mauritius for the applicant to be licensed
In determining an application, the Commission may take into account international standards, regulations, and information from foreign regulators

sections.source_quote: Where the Commission is satisfied that an application under section 10 ought to be granted, it shall grant the application and on payment of the prescribed fee, issue a licence authorising the applicant to carry on such category of insurance business in respect of such class of insurance policies as may be specified in the licence.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data and record-keeping requirements

sections.heading: Conditions of licence
sections.title: Conditions imposed by licensing authority on insurers
sections.summary: The Commission can impose conditions, restrictions, and limitations on insurers when issuing licenses. It may add, delete, vary, or determine these conditions via written notice to insurers, and the license is deemed issued with such changes. Insurers who fail to comply with these conditions face fines up to 2 million rupees and imprisonment for up to 8 years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the authority to impose and modify conditions on insurers, which could affect cross-border operations if conditions restrict international activities or require specific data handling practices.

requirements.text: The Commission may impose conditions, restrictions and limitations on insurers when issuing a licence
The Commission may add, delete, vary or determine conditions in the licence via written notice to the insurer
The licence is deemed issued with any additions, deletions, variations or determinations
Insurers failing to comply with conditions face fines up to 2 million rupees and imprisonment up to 8 years

sections.source_quote: The Commission may, on issuing a licence under this Part, impose such conditions, restrictions and limitations as it deems fit. Subject to Part XIII, the Commission may, by notice in writing to the insurer, add, delete, vary or determine any condition, restrictions and limitations in the licence and the licence shall be deemed to have been issued with such addition, deletion, variation or determination. Where an insurer fails to comply with a condition, restriction and limitation imposed under subsection (1), it shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding 2 million rupees and to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 8 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers and penalties for non-compliance

sections.heading: Branches and display of licence
sections.title: Mandatory display of insurance licence in branches
sections.summary: Insurers must display their licence with current conditions at all branches in Mauritius and provide copies to any branch office. They must also display the licence on request before business transactions. Failure to comply results in fines up to 500,000 rupees and imprisonment for up to 2 years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires insurers to maintain physical copies of their licences in branches and display them during transactions, which could impact data storage and cross-border operations if branches are located outside Mauritius.

requirements.text: Insurers must not open a branch in Mauritius without prior written notification to the Commission
Insurers must not open branches, agencies or acquire subsidiaries outside Mauritius without prior written notification to the Commission
Insurers must keep their licence at their principal place of business in Mauritius and a copy in every branch
Insurers must ensure the licence contains up-to-date conditions attached to the licence
Insurers must display the licence with up-to-date conditions on request before business transactions
Insurers failing to comply face fines up to 500,000 rupees and imprisonment up to 2 years

sections.source_quote: A person licensed to carry on insurance business under this Part – shall not without prior written notification to the Commission – open a branch in Mauritius; open a branch, agency or set up or acquire any subsidiary in any country outside Mauritius. shall keep its licence, at all times, at its principal place of business in Mauritius and a copy thereof in every branch or office that it may have; ensure that the licence kept in accordance with paragraph (i) contains the up-to-date conditions attached to the licence; display, on request made by any person on or before entering into a business transaction with that insurer, the licence together with the up-to-date conditions to the licence.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: branch licensing not directly data-related

sections.heading: Maintenance of financially sound condition
sections.title: Financial solvency requirements for insurers
sections.summary: Insurers must maintain financial solvency by meeting liabilities as they arise and keeping a solvency margin. They are deemed to contravene solvency rules if they fail to meet any solvency margin, technical provisions, or investment requirements. Officers must inform the Commission if they know or suspect the insurer is insolvent.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates financial solvency through margin and technical provisions, which could impact data storage and processing if financial stability affects data handling practices.

requirements.text: An insurer must maintain financial solvency by meeting liabilities as they arise
An insurer must maintain a solvency margin as required by solvency rules
An insurer must be deemed to contravene solvency rules if it fails to meet solvency margin, technical provisions, or investment requirements
An officer who knows or suspects an insurer is insolvent must inform the Commission in writing

sections.source_quote: An insurer shall maintain its business in a financially sound condition by generally conducting its affairs so as to be in a position at all times to – meet its liabilities as they arise; and keep the solvency margin required under this Part. An insurer shall, notwithstanding the circumstances which prevent it from maintaining a financially sound condition, be deemed to contravene subsection (1) where it fails to meet any requirement under this Act or any solvency rule relating to – the solvency margin and the method of its valuation and calculation; the maintenance of any technical provisions and reserves; and the kinds and spread of investment of assets.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial regulation, no data privacy aspects

sections.heading: Solvency margin
sections.title: Solvency margin maintenance and reporting
sections.summary: Insurers must maintain a solvency margin as specified in solvency rules and submit regular returns on this margin in the form, manner, and time specified by solvency rules.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires insurers to maintain a solvency margin and report on it, which could affect data storage and processing if financial stability is tied to data handling practices.

requirements.text: An insurer must maintain a solvency margin of the value specified in solvency rules
An insurer must submit returns on its solvency margin in the form, manner, and time specified by solvency rules

sections.source_quote: An insurer shall – at all times maintain a solvency margin of such value as may be specified in solvency rules; furnish to the Commission a return on its solvency margin in such form, manner and medium, and within such time of its balance sheet date, as may be specified in solvency rules.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data reporting requirements

sections.heading: Technical reserves
sections.title: Technical reserves and asset allocation
sections.summary: Insurers must make adequate technical provisions for underwriting liabilities, including unexpired risks and claims. They must hold unencumbered assets equal to technical provisions and ensure investments are diversified and spread according to solvency rules.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires insurers to maintain technical reserves and allocate assets, which could impact data storage and processing if financial stability affects data handling practices.

requirements.text: An insurer must make adequate technical provisions for underwriting liabilities
An insurer must hold unencumbered assets equal to technical provisions
An insurer must ensure investments are diversified and adequately spread according to solvency rules

sections.source_quote: An insurer shall make adequate technical provisions in its accounts for its underwriting liabilities in respect of its insurance policies, whether long term or general, as the case may be, including liabilities for unexpired risks, outstanding and incurred claims, provisions for claims incurred but not reported, and liabilities for policy benefits which have not become claimable, computed in accordance with a method specified in solvency rules. An insurer shall at all times hold unencumbered assets to the value of its technical provisions and after making adequate provisions for all its other liabilities.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential financial governance for insurers

sections.heading: Separation of fund
sections.title: Separation of insurance funds for policies
sections.summary: Insurers must maintain separate insurance funds for Mauritian and non-Mauritian policies, with specific rules for asset allocation, liability management, and fund independence. The Commission may allow a single fund if the number of policies is small.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates separate funds for different policy types, which could impact data storage and processing if data is held in different jurisdictions.

requirements.text: Insurers must set up and maintain separate insurance funds for Mauritian and non-Mauritian policies
The Commission may allow a single fund for both Mauritian and non-Mauritian policies if the number of policies is small
Insurers must maintain solvency margins, technical provisions, and reserves for each fund as if they were separate businesses
Insurers must pay into the corresponding insurance fund all money received for policies related to that fund
Insurers must carry all income and gains from investments of assets in the fund
Insurers must apply assets of a fund only to meet liabilities and expenses attributable to that fund

sections.source_quote: Subject to subsection (2), an insurer shall, regardless of the category of its insurance business, set up and maintain for so long as it is under liability in respect of an insurance policy or claim relating to that fund, separate insurance funds for its Mauritian policies and for its non-Mauritian policies. The Commission may having regard to the small number of insurance policies and their relative value in any one of the insurance fund, allow the insurer to maintain only one insurance fund for both the Mauritian and the non-Mauritian policies. An insurer shall – maintain the solvency margin, make such technical provisions and establish such reserve or fund, invest assets and apply the solvency rules in respect of each insurance fund as if they were separate businesses; pay into the corresponding insurance fund all money received by it in respect of policies to which the insurance fund relates; carry to the insurance fund all income and gains arising from the investment of the assets of that insurance fund.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: financial governance, not direct data compliance

sections.heading: Designated fund
sections.title: Designated fund for long-term insurance policies
sections.summary: Long-term insurers must maintain a designated fund for each class of long-term insurance business they carry. This fund must include all premiums received for policies of that class and investment income from the fund. The fund must be kept separate from other insurer assets and cannot include goodwill, capital expenditures, or assets excluded by FSC rules. Insurers cannot transfer fund assets for purposes other than claims and expenses related to that business, except from established surpluses.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires insurers to maintain segregated funds for specific insurance classes, with strict rules on fund usage to ensure financial stability during cross-border data transfers involving insurance policies.

requirements.text: A long-term insurer must keep a designated fund for each class of long-term insurance business it carries
The designated fund must include all premiums received for policies of that class
The designated fund must include all investment income from the fund
Assets of the designated fund must be kept separate from other insurer assets
The designated fund cannot include goodwill amounts
The designated fund cannot include capital expenditures
The designated fund cannot include assets excluded by FSC rules
Insurers cannot transfer fund assets for purposes other than claims and expenses related to that business
Insurers cannot transfer fund assets for purposes other than claims and expenses related to that part of the business
Insurers cannot transfer surplus from any designated fund to shareholders' funds unless all deficits in other designated funds have been met
Insurers cannot make transfers to shareholders' funds except from established surpluses
Insurers cannot declare or distribute dividends unless all deficits in designated funds have been met

sections.source_quote: A long term insurer shall keep within its corresponding insurance fund set up under section 17, an appropriately designated fund in respect of each class of long term insurance business carried on, into which shall be paid – (a) all money received by the insurer in respect of policies of that class which are issued by it or under which it has undertaken liability; (b) all income and gains arising from the investment of the assets of that designated fund.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial regulation, no data privacy or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Prohibitions concerning assets
sections.title: Prohibitions on insurer asset encumbrances
sections.summary: Insurers cannot mortgage, charge, or encumber their assets without Commission approval. They also cannot directly or indirectly borrow assets without Commission approval. Insurers may provide security only through guarantee policies they are authorized to issue under their license. Insurers cannot invest in derivatives except for specific purposes like linked long-term policies, risk reduction, or portfolio management.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts how insurers can use assets across borders, particularly for cross-border data transfers involving financial assets.

requirements.text: An insurer cannot mortgage, charge, or otherwise encumber its assets without Commission approval
An insurer cannot directly or indirectly borrow any asset without Commission approval
An insurer can provide security only through guarantee policies it is authorized to issue under its license
An insurer cannot invest in derivatives except for linked long-term policies
An insurer can invest in derivatives for risk reduction or efficient portfolio management
An insurer can invest in derivatives if they will have the asset at the settlement date matching obligations

sections.source_quote: An insurer shall not, without the approval of the Commission, mortgage, charge or otherwise encumber its assets; directly or indirectly borrow any asset; or by means of any surety, give any security in relation to obligations between other persons except where the security is provided under a guarantee policy which the insurer is authorised to issue under its licence.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: financial governance, not direct data compliance

sections.heading: Examination of reinsurance treaties
sections.title: Reinsurance treaty examinations
sections.summary: Insurers must enter into reinsurance treaties consistent with sound insurance principles. They must submit reinsurance treaties to the Commission for examination. The Commission can terminate or modify treaties if they are unfavorable to the insurer, not technically appropriate, or involve unfit reinsurance brokers. The Commission considers insurer business category, premium amounts, asset value, and reinsurer quality when making decisions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires insurers to have reinsurance treaties reviewed by the Commission, which impacts data transfers involving international reinsurance arrangements.

requirements.text: An insurer must enter into reinsurance treaties consistent with sound insurance business principles
An insurer must submit reinsurance treaties to the Commission for examination

sections.heading: Remedial measures
sections.title: Remedial measures for solvency breaches
sections.summary: If an insurer's solvency margin is below required levels, they cannot assume new risks, declare dividends, or remit profits out of Mauritius without Commission approval. Insurers must make good deficiencies in solvency. The Chief Executive can direct insurers to submit information on failures, actuary reports, and recovery plans. The Chief Executive may appoint administrators or revoke licenses if solvency is compromised.

sections.heading: Stated capital and deposit
sections.title: Stated capital and deposit requirements
sections.summary: Insurers must maintain stated capital and a deposit with a custodian as prescribed. They must make good any impaired stated capital or deposit below prescribed amounts. Deposits cannot be transferred, encumbered, or used for liabilities except for Mauritian policies. Insurers may maintain minimum capital in currencies other than the Mauritian rupee with Commission approval.

sections.heading: Shares and debentures
sections.title: Restrictions on issuing preferential shares and debentures
sections.summary: An insurer cannot issue or convert shares with preferential distribution rights, convert shares between classes, convert shares to debentures, issue debentures, or reduce its minimum capital without Commission approval. These restrictions apply to insurers in Mauritius and are designed to maintain capital adequacy and prevent financial instability.

sections.title: Prohibition on nominee share registration without approval

sections.heading: Limitation on control of insurers
sections.title: Shareholding limits to prevent control concentration
sections.summary: No person can acquire significant shareholding without Commission approval, with conditions including fitness, policyholder interests, and public interest. sections.heading: Effect of registration of shares contrary to Act
sections.title: Consequences of unlawful share registration
sections.summary: Shareholders cannot receive dividends, vote, or be allotted shares in contravention of section 27. Resolutions passed by insurers are not invalidated by such votes, and restitution can be sought from courts for dividends or shares received unlawfully.

sections.heading: Furnishing of information on shareholding
sections.title: Mandatory shareholding information disclosure
sections.summary: The Commission may require insurers to disclose shareholder information, nominee details, and persons exercising shareholder rights. Shareholders and insurers must provide this information upon request, with penalties for non-compliance.

sections.heading: Management of an insurer
sections.title: Insurer management and governance requirements
sections.summary: Insurers must be managed by appointed persons under their constitutive documents, authorized agents with Commission approval, or licensed insurance managers. Boards must have at least 7 directors with 30% independent directors, defined as those without material relationships affecting their judgment.

sections.heading: Duties of directors
sections.title: Directors' duties for insurer governance and stakeholder interests
sections.summary: Directors of insurers must consider stakeholders' interests, establish robust internal controls aligned with sound governance and risk management, monitor compliance with regulatory requirements, set up necessary committees, approve financial sustainability policies, and implement conflict of interest and confidentiality protocols. These obligations apply to all insurer directors and must be fulfilled to ensure the insurer operates responsibly and maintains stakeholder trust.

sections.source_quote: Duties of directors (1) Without limiting their duties in managing the affairs of an insurer, the directors shall – (a) have regard to the interests of its stakeholders, including its policyholders; (b) establish adequate internal controls and adopt strategies, policies, processes and procedures in accordance with principles of sound corporate governance and risk management; (c) monitor compliance with the laws, rules, directions and guidelines of the Commission, and with the policies adopted; (d) set up such committees as they deem necessary to discharge their responsibilities effectively; (e) approve policies regarding the maintenance of the undertaking in a sound financial situation in accordance with section 14; (f) establish and maintain proper policies for – (i) identifying and resolving situations of, or potential situations of, conflict of interest; (ii) restricting the use of confidential information; (iii) the disclosure of relevant information to clients so as to allow them to make informed decisions; (iv) dealing with complaints from policyholders or members of the public in general; and (v) such other matters as to ensure sound and sustainable conduct of business.

sections.heading: Avoiding conflict of interest
sections.title: Disclosure of conflicts of interest in major contracts
sections.summary: Officers of insurers must disclose material interests in major contracts to the Commission within specific timeframes, including when they become aware of the contract. Directors must disclose at board meetings where contracts are considered. Failure to disclose constitutes an offence with penalties up to one million rupees and three years imprisonment.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires disclosure of conflicts of interest in cross-border data transfers, ensuring transparency in data handling by officers involved with international contracts.

sections.source_quote: Avoiding conflict of interest (1) Subject to subsections (2) and (3), an officer of an insurer who – (a) is a party to a major contract or proposed major contract with the insurer; (b) is an officer or a substantial shareholder of any entity that is a party to a major contract or proposed major contract with the insurer; or (c) has a material interest in any person who is a party to a major contract or proposed major contract with the insurer, shall, immediately after becoming aware of the contract or proposed contract, disclose in writing or cause to be entered in a record kept for that purpose by the insurer the nature and extent of that interest in accordance with the other provisions of this section.

sections.heading: Requirement to abstain
sections.title: Abstention from meetings when conflicted
sections.summary: Directors must abstain from meetings while considering contracts where they have a conflict of interest, except for specific categories like security arrangements, remuneration contracts, or affiliate contracts. Failure to abstain results in fines up to one million rupees and imprisonment for up to three years, with potential disqualification from director roles for five years.

sections.source_quote: Requirement to abstain (1) Where section 32 applies to a director in respect of a major contract, the director shall not be present at any meeting of directors while the contract is being considered at the meeting or vote on any resolution to approve the contract unless the contract is – (a) an arrangement by way of security for money lent to, or obligations undertaken by the director for the benefit of, the insurer or a subsidiary of the insurer; (b) a contract relating primarily to the director's remuneration as a director or an officer, employee or agent of the insurer or a subsidiary of the insurer or an entity controlled by the insurer or an entity in which the insurer has a material investment; or (c) a contract with an affiliate of the insurer.

sections.heading: Consequences of conflict of interest
sections.title: Validity of contracts with conflicts of interest
sections.summary: Contracts involving conflicts of interest are not void solely due to the relationship, but may be invalidated if the officer failed to disclose the interest, the contract was not approved by directors, or it was not in the insurer's interest at approval. This ensures contracts remain valid while maintaining accountability.
sections.dtia_summary: Contracts involving conflicts of interest remain valid unless specific conditions are met, which impacts data transfer by requiring disclosure and approval processes for cross-border data handling.

sections.source_quote: Consequences of conflict of interest (1) Notwithstanding sections 32 and 33, and subject to subsection (2), a contract between an insurer and one or more of its officers, or between an insurer and another entity of which an officer of the insurer is a director or an officer or between an insurer and a person in which the officer has a material interest, shall not be void – (a) by reason only of that relationship; or (b) by reason only that a director with an interest in the contract is present at or is counted to determine the presence of a quorum at the meeting of directors or the committee of directors that authorised the contract.

sections.heading: Shareholder action

sections.heading: Internal control systems

Insurance brokers must disclose commissions or remuneration to prospective policyholders before policy entry

sections.source_quote: No insurance broker shall provide its services in relation to an insurance policy where to do so would result or be likely to result in a conflict of interest between the broker and an insurer or a policyholder.
sections.relevance_reason: Conflicts of interest disclosure for financial data

sections.heading: Registration of insurance salespersons
sections.title: Registration of insurance salespersons
sections.summary: Insurance salespersons must be registered with the Commission before acting for insurers or agents. The Commission maintains a register of salespersons, and insurers must apply for registration before appointing salespersons. The Commission may terminate registration if a salesperson is deemed unfit or fails to pay annual fees.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires registration of salespersons to ensure compliance with financial data handling standards in cross-border insurance operations.

requirements.text: No person may act as insurance salesperson without Commission registration
Commission must maintain a register of insurance salespersons
Insurers must apply to Commission for registration before appointing salespersons
Applications must include proof of fitness and proper documentation
Commission may refuse registration or terminate appointments of unfit salespersons
Insurers must notify Commission within 14 days of terminating salesperson appointments

sections.source_quote: No person other than a natural person shall act as insurance salesperson for an insurer or insurance agent unless that person is registered with the Commission to act as such for that particular insurer or insurance agent.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers and registration requirements

sections.heading: Annual fee for insurance salespersons
sections.title: Annual fee for insurance salespersons
sections.summary: Insurers and agents must pay annual fees for insurance salespersons as prescribed by the Commission. Failure to pay within one month of the due date results in registration lapse and removal from the Commission's register. Insurers must maintain updated lists of salespersons and provide copies to the Commission upon request.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely payment of annual fees to maintain salesperson registration, ensuring financial accountability in cross-border insurance operations.

requirements.text: Annual fee must be paid by insurer or agent within prescribed timeframe
Registration lapses if annual fee remains unpaid for one month
Salespersons whose registration lapses must not act for insurers or agents
Insurers must maintain updated lists of salespersons and provide copies to Commission

sections.source_quote: There shall be prescribed an annual fee to be paid in respect of every insurance salesperson.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: general compliance with registration and fees

sections.heading: Annual fee for insurance salespersons

Failure to pay within one month of the due date results in registration lapse and removal from the Commission's register. Insurers must maintain updated lists of salespersons and provide copies to the Commission upon request.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely payment of annual fees to maintain salesperson registration, ensuring financial accountability in cross-border insurance operations.

requirements.text: Annual fee must be paid by insurer or agent within prescribed timeframe
Registration lapses if annual fee remains unpaid for one month
