sections.heading: Claims professionals
sections.title: Registration of claims professionals
sections.summary: The Minister may regulate the registration and business conduct of claims professionals. A claims professional is defined as someone who advises on or investigates losses, assembles loss data, or assesses claims for general insurance policies for a fee. This section establishes the regulatory framework for claims professionals operating within the insurance sector.

requirements.text: The Minister may by regulations provide for the registration of claim professionals and the conduct of their business

sections.source_quote: 78. The Minister may by regulations provide for the registration of claim professionals and the conduct of their business.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory framework for claims professionals

sections.heading: Certain provisions to be void
sections.title: Void provisions in insurance agreements
sections.summary: Certain clauses in insurance agreements are void if they exempt insurers from liability for actions of agents, allow policyholders to waive rights under the Act, or make policy benefits dependent on reinsurance obligations. This section specifies which contractual terms are automatically invalid to protect policyholders from unfair practices.

requirements.text: A provision of an agreement shall be void where the purport of the provision is that an insurer is exempted from liability for the actions, omissions or representations of a person acting on its behalf in relation to an insurance policy

A provision of an agreement shall be void where the person who has entered into the insurance policy declares or admits that a person who acted on behalf of the insurer in connection with an offer of that person to do so was in fact appointed to act on behalf of the first-mentioned person
A provision of an agreement shall be void where the obligation of an insurer under an insurance policy is dependent upon the discharging of an obligation of another person under a reinsurance treaty

A provision of an agreement shall be void where the person who has entered into an insurance policy, or the life insured, or any beneficiary, under an insurance policy waives a right to which he is, by or under this Act, entitled

sections.source_quote: 79. A provision of an agreement shall be void, where the purport of the provision is that- (a) an insurer is exempted from liability for the actions, omissions or representations of a person acting on its behalf in relation to an insurance policy; (b) the person who has entered into the insurance policy declares or admits that a person who acted on behalf of the insurer in connection with an offer of that person to do so, or with the negotiations preceding the entering into it, was in fact appointed to act on behalf of the first-mentioned person; (c) the obligation of an insurer under an insurance policy is dependent upon the discharging of an obligation of another person under a reinsurance treaty; (d) the person who has entered into an insurance policy, or the life insured, or any beneficiary, under an insurance policy, waives a right to which he is, by or under this Act, entitled.
sections.relevance_reason: void clauses protect policyholders from unfair terms

sections.heading: Information on policies
sections.title: Policyholder information requirements
sections.summary: Insurers must provide policyholders with a written summary of key policy details within 30 days of policy inception or amendment. The summary must include material representations made by the policyholder, premiums, benefits, fees, and conditions for policy benefits. Insurers face penalties for false or misleading summaries. This section ensures transparency in policy terms and conditions.

requirements.text: The insurer or insurance agent shall provide written information in the form of a summary relating to at least the following matters: (a) representations made by or on behalf of the policyholder; (b) premiums payable and policy benefits; (c) disclosure of fees, commissions and other costs; (d) events for which policy benefits are provided
The summary may be used in evidence where relevant to determine matters relating to the policy
The summary shall be deemed representative of matters material to risk assessment in the absence of contrary evidence
An insurer or agent who knowingly provides a false or misleading summary shall be liable to a fine of up to one million rupees or imprisonment for up to five years

sections.source_quote: 80. Where an insurance policy, other than a reinsurance treaty, is entered into or varied, the insurer, or the insurance agent, shall, not later than 30 days after the policy has been entered into, provide for the purpose of the policyholder written information, in the form of a summary, relating to at least the following matters, namely - (a) those of the representations made by or on behalf of the policyholder to the insurer which were regarded by that insurer as material to its assessment of the risks under the policy; (b) the premiums payable and the policy benefits to be provided under the policy; (c) the disclosure of fees, commissions and other costs on the policy; (d) the events in respect of which the policy benefits are to be provided and the circumstances, if any, in which those benefits are not to be provided.
sections.relevance_reason: Policyholder transparency and data disclosure

sections.heading: Free choice of policy
sections.title: Free choice of insurance policy for debt security
sections.summary: Policyholders have the right to choose insurance policies for debt security without restriction on insurers, policy benefits, or aggregate value. This section ensures policyholders can select policies that meet their debt obligations without being forced into specific terms. Policyholders must confirm written consent before using policies for debt security.

requirements.text: The person who requires insurance for debt security shall have free choice as to whether to enter into a new policy or use an existing policy
The person who requires insurance for debt security shall have free choice as to the insurer and insurance agent, if any
The person who requires insurance for debt security shall have free choice as to whether policy benefits are provided in events other than death or disability
The person who requires insurance for debt security shall have free choice as to whether the aggregate value of policy benefits exceeds the debt obligation
The policyholder must confirm in writing prior to using the policy for debt security that they were given prior written notification of their freedom of choice
The policyholder must freely and willingly exercise their freedom of choice
Policy benefits accrued under the policy shall be paid to the creditor only to the value of the creditor's interest, with any surplus paid to the policyholder
Where provisions of subsections (1) and (3) are not complied with, the security provided by the policy shall be void and policy benefits shall be paid to the policyholder

sections.source_quote: 81. Where a party to a contract in terms of which money is loaned, goods are leased or credit is granted requires, whether as a condition thereof or otherwise, that an insurance policy or its policy benefits be made available and used for the purpose of protecting the interests of a creditor, the person who is so required to make that policy or those policy benefits available shall have a free choice - (a) as to whether he wishes to enter into a new policy and make it available for that purpose, or wishes to make available an existing policy of the appropriate value for that purpose, or wishes to utilize a combination of those options; (b) where a new policy is to be entered into – (i) as to the insurer with which the policy is entered into and as to the insurance agent, if any; (ii) as to whether or not the policy benefits concerned are to be provided in an event other than the death or disability of the life insured; (iii) as to whether or not the value of the policy benefits to be provided thereunder, when taken in the aggregate with the value of the policy benefits provided under any other policy which is also to be made available and used for that purpose, shall exceed the value of that debt or other obligation; (c) where an existing policy is to be made available, as to whether or not a variation of the policy required for that purpose shall be such as to cause – (i) policy benefits to be provided in an event other than the death or disability of the life insured; (ii) the value of the policy benefits to be provided thereunder, when taken in the aggregate with the value of the policy benefits provided under any other policy which is also to be made available and used for that purpose, to exceed the value of that debt or other obligation.
sections.relevance_reason: policyholder consent and debt security terms

sections.heading: Annual statement to certain policyholders
sections.title: Annual performance reports for investment-linked policies
sections.summary: Insurers must provide policyholders with annual reports on the performance of 'with profit' life insurance products and investment-linked policies. The Commission may specify the format and content of these reports. This section ensures transparency in investment performance for policyholders.

requirements.text: Where a policyholder has invested in a 'with profit' life insurance product which entitles him to a bonus at the discretion of the insurer or in an investment linked policy, the insurer shall provide the policyholder with a report of the performance of the investment in such products, regularly and in any case not less than once every year

sections.source_quote: 82. Where a policyholder has invested in a "with profit" life insurance product which entitles him to a bonus at the discretion of the insurer or in an investment linked policy, the insurer shall provide the policyholder with a report of the performance of the investment in such products, regularly and in any case not less than once every year.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator's role in report format

sections.heading: Undesirable business practice
sections.title: Undesirable insurance business practices
sections.summary: The Commission may declare certain insurance business practices as undesirable for specific policies or categories of persons. Insurers or service providers carrying out such practices must suspend them or take remedial actions. Failure to comply results in fines up to one million rupees and imprisonment for up to three years.

requirements.text: The Commission may declare a particular insurance business practice to be undesirable for all or a particular class of insurance policies
The Commission may declare a particular insurance business practice to be undesirable for all or a particular category of persons who render services in respect of insurance policies

Where the Commission is satisfied that an insurer or person rendering services is carrying on an undesirable business practice, the Commission may direct that insurer or person to suspend the practice for a period as deemed necessary

Where the Commission is satisfied that an insurer or person rendering services is carrying on an undesirable business practice, the Commission may direct that insurer or person to take remedial actions to the satisfaction of the Commission

An insurer or other person who carries on a practice declared undesirable under this section shall commit an offence and be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and imprisonment for up to three years

sections.source_quote: 83. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in any enactment, the Commission may declare a particular insurance business practice to be undesirable for - (a) all or a particular class of insurance policies; or (b) all or a particular category of persons who render services in respect of insurance policies.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers and penalties for data practices

sections.heading: Abandoned funds
sections.title: Abandoned insurance funds after 10 years of inactivity
sections.summary: If an insurance policyholder's entitlement to funds remains unclaimed for 10+ years, the insurer must send a notice via registered post to the last known address and publish it in the Gazette and two newspapers. If unresponsive, the entitlement is deemed abandoned and transferred to the Curator of Vacant Estates without further action.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires notification and publication before transferring unclaimed funds to a curator, relevant for DTIA when assessing disposal of dormant assets across borders.

requirements.text: Send notice by registered post to policyholder's last known address
Publish notice in Gazette and two daily newspapers
Deem entitlement abandoned if no response within 10+ years
Transfer abandoned entitlement to Curator of Vacant Estates

sections.source_quote: 84. Abandoned funds (1) Notwithstanding anything in any agreement between an insurer and a policyholder, and irrespective of the amount, where a policyholder's entitlement to receive money under an insurance policy has been left untouched and not claimed for 10 years or more, the insurer shall - (a) send by registered post a notice to that effect to the last known address of the policyholder or the beneficiary under the policy; (b) cause the notice to be published in the Gazette and in two daily newspapers.
sections.relevance_reason: Publishes notice in media, affects data retention

sections.heading: Protection of policyholders
sections.title: Policyholder protection through FSC guidelines
sections.summary: The Financial Services Commission may issue rules and guidelines for sound insurance practices, including disclosure of fees, advertising standards, information requirements for contracts, and other matters affecting insurance business conduct. These rules are subject to the Code Civil Mauricien.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines regulatory oversight for policyholder information practices, relevant for DTIA when transferring data across borders involving insurance contracts.

requirements.text: FSC may provide rules and guidelines for sound insurance principles and practice
FSC rules may include disclosure of fees, commissions and costs on policies
FSC rules may include advertising and canvassing standards for insurance policies
FSC rules may include information requirements before and after insurance contracts
FSC rules may include other matters affecting insurance business conduct

sections.source_quote: 85. Protection of policyholders (1) Without prejudice to the provisions of Livre Troisième, Titre Douzième, Chapitre Troisième of the Code Civil Mauricien, the Commission may provide in the FSC Rules and guidelines for sound insurance principles and practice.
sections.relevance_reason: FSC rules on disclosure and contracts affect data sharing

sections.heading: Assumption of risk by general insurer
sections.title: Premium payment requirements for insurers
sections.summary: General insurers must receive premiums in prescribed manner and within prescribed time. If a third party receives premiums, they must remit to the insurer within prescribed time. Postal money orders and cheques are treated as payment if honoured, and premiums paid before risk commencement to agents are treated as paid to the insurer.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets rules for premium receipt and payment, relevant for DTIA when assessing cross-border financial transactions involving insurance premiums.

requirements.text: General insurer must receive premiums in prescribed manner and within prescribed time
If third party receives premium, insurer bears proof of unauthorized receipt
Third party must remit premium to insurer within prescribed time
Postal money order and cheque dates treated as payment if honoured
Premium paid before risk commencement to agent treated as payment to insurer

sections.source_quote: 86. Assumption of risk by general insurer (1) No general insurer shall assume any risk in respect of such general insurance policy as may be prescribed except where the premium payable is received by the insurer in such manner and within such time as may be prescribed.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential financial procedure for premiums

sections.heading: Premium rate under long term policy
sections.title: Long-term insurance premium certification
sections.summary: Long-term insurers must certify premium rates as prudentially sound by an actuary. They must provide Commission with product details, actuary's certificate, and sales materials at least 30 days before public offering. The Commission may prohibit or require changes to products if unsuitable, and actuarial reports must be submitted for suitability.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires actuarial certification for premium rates, relevant for DTIA when assessing financial data transfers for long-term insurance products.

requirements.text: Long-term insurer must certify premium rates as prudentially sound
Long-term insurer must provide Commission with product details and actuary's certificate 30 days before public offering
Commission may prohibit product offering if deemed inappropriate
Commission may require changes to product before public offering
Actuary must certify premium rates as suitable and in accordance with sound principles
Commission may require actuary to provide report on policy terms and premium rates
Commission may take measures based on actuary's report

sections.source_quote: 87. Premium rate under long term policy (1) A long term insurer shall not issue a long term insurance policy except where the premium rate chargeable under that class of policy has been certified by the actuary as being prudentially sound.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers and financial data handling

sections.heading: Compensation Fund
sections.title: Insurance industry compensation fund
sections.summary: A Compensation Fund must be established under regulations to pay claims for insolvent insurers and personal injury compensation in traffic accidents where the tortfeasor is untraceable. The fund may be organized into sub-funds for different policy classes and administered per regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: Establishes a fund for claims from insolvent insurers, relevant for DTIA when assessing cross-border financial liabilities.

requirements.text: Establish Compensation Fund under regulations with powers of a natural person
Fund must pay claims for insolvent insurers' unpaid policies
Fund must pay compensation for traffic injuries where tortfeasor is untraceable
Fund may be organized into sub-funds for different policy classes
Fund must be administered per regulations

sections.source_quote: 88. Compensation Fund (1) There shall be established, under regulations made by the Minister, a fund to be known as the Insurance Industry Compensation Fund which shall have all the powers of a natural person- (a) for the payment of any claims, in respect of risks situated in Mauritius as may be prescribed, against an insurer licensed under this Act, remaining unpaid by reason of insolvency of such insurer, subject to such limitations, restrictions, exclusions, and conditions as may be prescribed; and (b) for the payment of compensation to persons suffering personal injury in traffic accidents where the tortfeasor or the vehicle which caused the injury is untraceable, subject to such limitations and restrictions as may be prescribed.
sections.relevance_reason: Compensation fund structure for financial claims

sections.heading: Contribution to the Compensation Fund
sections.title: Contribution to compensation fund
sections.summary: Insurers must contribute to the Compensation Fund based on their insurance business in Mauritius, with amounts and timing prescribed by regulations. Contributions cannot be used for substantial shareholders or officers of insolvent insurers with excessive policy benefits.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets contribution rules for compensation funds, relevant for DTIA when assessing financial data transfers involving insolvency claims.

requirements.text: Insurer must contribute to Compensation Fund based on insurance business in Mauritius
Contributions may be prescribed for different categories of insurance business
No payment from fund to substantial shareholders of insolvent insurer
No payment from fund to officers of insolvent insurer with excessive policy benefits

sections.source_quote: 89. Contribution to the Compensation Fund (1) An insurer shall, to the extent of its insurance business relating to Mauritian policies, contribute to the Compensation Fund in such amounts payable at such time as may be prescribed and different amounts may be prescribed with respect to different categories of insurance business or different classes or part classes of policies, or in view of different circumstances.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory contributions with financial data restrictions

sections.heading: Management of the Compensation Fund
sections.title: Compensation Fund Management Committee Structure
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Compensation Fund be managed by a committee of up to seven members appointed by the Minister. The committee must maintain separate accounts for any sub-funds established, and payments into a sub-fund must be used exclusively for compensation related to that sub-fund after administrative costs are deducted.

requirements.text: The Compensation Fund shall be managed by a managing committee consisting of not more than 7 persons appointed by the Minister.
Separate accounts shall be kept by the managing committee in respect of each sub-fund.

Payments made into one sub-fund, after deduction of any amount determined by the managing committee to cover any administrative costs, shall be utilised exclusively for compensation to meet only the purposes of that sub-fund.

sections.source_quote: 90. Management of the Compensation Fund (1) The Compensation Fund shall be managed by a managing committee consisting of not more than 7 persons appointed by the Minister.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial governance, not direct data compliance

sections.heading: Prescription of claims
sections.title: Claim Prescription Periods for Compensation Fund
sections.summary: This section specifies the time limits for claiming compensation from the fund. Claims related to traffic accidents where the tortfeasor or vehicle is untraceable must be made within two years of the accident. All other claims must be made within two years of the insurer being removed from the register or wound up.

requirements.text: No claim for payment of compensation shall be made out of the Compensation Fund except where the claim is made in the case of claims made by persons suffering personal injury in traffic accidents where the tortfeasor or the vehicle which caused the injury is untraceable, within 2 years from the date of the occurrence of the accident relating to that claim.

No claim for payment of compensation shall be made out of the Compensation Fund except where the claim is made in any other case, within 2 years from the date the name of the insurer is removed from the register or the insurer is definitely wound up.

sections.source_quote: 91. Prescription of claims No claim for payment of compensation shall be made out of the Compensation Fund except where the claim is made – (a) in the case of claims made by persons suffering personal injury in traffic accidents where the tortfeasor or the vehicle which caused the injury is untraceable, within 2 years from the date of the occurrence of the accident relating to that claim; (b) in any other case, within 2 years from the date the name of the insurer is removed from the register or the insurer is definitely wound up.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Regulations on Compensation Fund
sections.title: Compensation Fund Regulatory Framework
sections.summary: This section allows regulations to be made under the Act for financing the Compensation Fund, including contributions from licensees, subscription powers, determining compensation rights, claim settlement, compensation quantum, subrogation rights, payment terms, and accounting requirements.

requirements.text: Regulations may provide for the levying of contributions from licensees and other means of financing the Compensation Fund.
Regulations may provide for the power to subscribe to insurance policies.
Regulations may provide for the mode of determining the right to compensation payable under the Compensation Fund and the circumstances in which such right may be excluded or modified.
Regulations may provide for the power to settle claims.
Regulations may provide for specifications as to the quantum of the compensation.
Regulations may provide for the right of subrogation to the Compensation Fund in order to recover from any person whose liability is extinguished or reduced by the payment of the compensation.
Regulations may provide for the terms and conditions on which compensation is to be payable.
Regulations may provide for accounts to be kept in respect of the Compensation Fund.

sections.source_quote: 92. Regulations on Compensation Fund Subject to this Act, regulations made under this Act may provide for - (a) the levying of contributions from licensees and other means of financing the Compensation Fund; (b) the power to subscribe to insurance policies; (c) the mode of determining the right to compensation payable under the Compensation Fund and the circumstances in which such right may be excluded or modified; (d) the power to settle claims; (e) specifications as to the quantum of the compensation; (f) the right of subrogation to the Compensation Fund in order to recover from any person whose liability is extinguished or reduced by the payment of the compensation; (g) the terms and conditions on which compensation is to be payable; and (h) accounts to be kept in respect of the Compensation Fund.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: financial data handling without direct data protection focus

sections.heading: Power of Commission to issue direction
sections.title: Commission's Power to Issue Compliance Directions
sections.summary: This section grants the Commission the authority to direct licensees or registered persons to comply with the Act, FSC Rules, or guidelines if they are not adhering to them. Non-compliance with such directions constitutes an offense punishable by a fine up to one million rupees and imprisonment for up to three years.

requirements.text: Where the Commission is of the view that a licensee or registered person is not complying with any provision of this Act, FSC Rule or guideline, it may direct the licensee or registered person to comply with such provision, FSC Rule or guideline.

A licensee or registered person who fails to comply with a direction issued under subsection (1) shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 3 years.

sections.source_quote: 93. Power of Commission to issue direction (1) Without prejudice to the powers of the Commission under the Financial Services Act 2007, where the Commission is of the view that a licensee or registered person is not complying with any provision of this Act, FSC Rule or guideline, it may direct the licensee or registered person to comply with such provision, FSC Rule or guideline. (2) A licensee or registered person who fails to comply with a direction issued under subsection (1) shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 3 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for compliance enforcement

sections.heading: Request of information
sections.title: Information Request Powers for Insurers
sections.summary: This section empowers the Chief Executive to require licensees or registered persons to provide information or documents for Commission investigations. The information must be verified, and the power extends to any person in possession of the information. Non-compliance results in fines up to 500,000 rupees and imprisonment for up to two years.

requirements.text: The Chief Executive may, for the purpose of carrying out the functions of the Commission under this Act or any FSC Rules, by notice in writing, require a licensee or registered person to provide to the Commission – at such times or intervals, at such place and in respect of such periods with such information or document as may be specified in the notice.
The notice under subsection (1) may require the information or document to be verified in a manner or by a person referred to in subsection (1) (b).

The power to require production of information and document under subsection (1) shall extend to any person who, in the opinion of the Chief Executive, appears to be in possession of such information and document.

Where documents are produced, the Chief Executive may take copies of or extracts from them and require – any person who is a present or past officer, controller, partner, manager or employee of that licensee; the person producing the document; in the case of an insurer, any insurance agent, broker, manager or insurance salesperson who acts, or has acted for that insurer, to provide explanation on the document produced.

Where the information or document requested is not in the possession of the person who was required to produce it, that person shall state, to the best of his knowledge and belief, where the document can be found.

Any person who, without reasonable excuse, fails to comply with any requirement of this section shall commit an offence and shall on conviction be liable to a fine not exceeding 500,000 rupees and to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 2 years.

sections.source_quote: 94. Request of information (1) Without prejudice to the powers of the Chief Executive under the Financial Services Act 2007, the Chief Executive may, for the purpose of carrying out the functions of the Commission under this Act or any FSC Rules, by notice in writing, require a licensee or registered person to provide to the Commission – (a) at such times or intervals, at such place and in respect of such periods with such information or document as may be specified in the notice; (c) with a report, in such form as may be specified in the notice, by a person who has relevant professional skill and who is nominated or approved by the Commission on, or on any aspect of, any matter in relation to which the Chief Executive may require information under paragraph (a). (2) The notice under subsection (1) may require the information or document to be verified in a manner or by a person referred to in subsection (1) (b). (3) The power to require production of information and document under subsection (1) shall extend to any person who, in the opinion of the Chief Executive, appears to be in possession of such information and document. (4) Where documents are produced, the Chief Executive may take copies of or extracts from them and require – (a) any person who is a present or past officer, controller, partner, manager or employee of that licensee; (b) the person producing the document; (c) in the case of an insurer, any insurance agent, broker, manager or insurance salesperson who acts, or has acted for that insurer, to provide explanation on the document produced. (5) Where the information or document requested is not in the possession of the person who was required to produce it, that person shall state, to the best of his knowledge and belief, where the document can be found. (6) Any person who, without reasonable excuse, fails to comply with any requirement of this section shall commit an offence and shall on conviction be liable to a fine not exceeding 500,000 rupees and to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 2 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for data requests

sections.heading: On-site inspection
sections.title: On-site Inspection Authority for Insurers
sections.summary: This section grants the Chief Executive the authority to conduct on-site inspections of licensees or registered persons to verify compliance with sound insurance principles. Inspectors may request information, enter premises, examine documents, and require explanations. Obstruction of inspections results in fines up to 500,000 rupees and imprisonment for up to two years.

requirements.text: The Chief Executive may, at any time, inspect the affairs or any part of the affairs of a licensee or registered person in order to verify whether the licensee or registered person, as the case may be, is conducting its business according to sound insurance principles.

For the purpose of carrying out an inspection under subsection (1), the Chief Executive may appoint as inspector any person, whether or not a member of staff of the Commission, who is an auditor, actuary or any person of required competence.

An inspection may take place at the head office and any branch offices or any premises where business of the licensee or registered person is conducted or records are maintained by the licensee, registered person or any associated party.

For the purpose of the inspection, the inspector may – request such information and documents in such form and medium, put such questions and require such explanations as the inspector thinks necessary; enter any premises used or apparently used by the licensee for business purposes; examine, and make copies of or take extracts from documents; record any information or make copy of any information kept in electronic form; seek information, explanation or clarification about any document or information; and make such request as would assist him in the inspection.

A licensee, registered person or associated party shall, for the purpose of an inspection under this Part, provide the inspector with such facility and assistance as may be necessary for him to carry out the inspection in an efficient and effective manner.

Any person who obstructs an inspector in the performance of his duties under this section shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding 500,000 rupees and to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 2 years.

sections.source_quote: 95. On-site inspection (1) Without prejudice to the powers of the Chief Executive under the Financial Services Act 2007, the Chief Executive may, at any time, inspect the affairs or any part of the affairs of a licensee or registered person in order to verify whether the licensee or registered person, as the case may be, is conducting its business according to sound insurance principles (2) For the purpose of carrying out an inspection under subsection (1), the Chief Executive may appoint as inspector any person, whether or not a member of staff of the Commission, who is an auditor, actuary or any person of required competence. (3) An inspection may take place at the head office and any branch offices or any premises where business of the licensee or registered person is conducted or records are maintained by the licensee, registered person or any associated party. (4) For the purpose of the inspection, the inspector may – (a) request such information and documents in such form and medium, put such questions and require such explanations as the inspector thinks necessary; (b) enter any premises used or apparently used by the licensee for business purposes; (c) examine, and make copies of or take extracts from documents; (d) record any information or make copy of any information kept in electronic form; (e) seek information, explanation or clarification about any document or information; and (f) make such request as would assist him in the inspection. (5) A licensee, registered person or associated party shall, for the purpose of an inspection under this Part, provide the inspector with such facility and assistance as may be necessary for him to carry out the inspection in an efficient and effective manner.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for inspections

sections.heading: Investigation
sections.title: Investigation powers for financial regulators
sections.summary: The Chief Executive may investigate a licensee or registered person if they have failed to comply with Commission directions, committed fraud, pose serious risks to customers or financial stability, or failed to provide requested information. The investigation allows the regulator to require attendance, answers, and document production from the licensee and their officers. The regulator may take copies of documents and require explanations. Computer-stored information must be accessible in a visible, legible format. Failure to comply without reasonable excuse is an offence punishable by fines up to 1 million rupees and imprisonment for up to 3 years.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulators can compel data access and production during investigations, requiring data to be in a visible format for computer-stored information. This impacts data transfer and storage across borders as it may involve cross-border data access requests.

requirements.text: Chief Executive may investigate licensee or registered person for failure to comply with Commission directions
Chief Executive may require licensee to attend and answer questions or produce documents
Chief Executive may require any officer to furnish information or produce documents
Chief Executive may take copies or extracts from documents produced
Computer-stored information must be accessible in visible, legible format
Person required to answer questions or produce documents must not fail without reasonable excuse
Refusal to answer questions or produce documents may be excused if it would incriminate
Investigation may occur at premises where business is conducted or records are maintained
Chief Executive may appoint staff or others as investigators with powers of Chief Executive
Investigator may require documents, explanations, or attendance under oath
Failure to comply with investigation requirements is an offence punishable by fine up to 1 million rupees and imprisonment up to 3 years

sections.source_quote: Without prejudice to the powers of the Chief Executive under the Financial Services Act 2007, where the Chief Executive has reason to believe that a licensee or registered person – (a) has failed, is failing or is likely to fail, to comply with any direction issued by the Commission under section 93(1); (b) has committed, is committing or is likely to commit a fraud, any financial crime or a serious misconduct; (c) has carried on, is carrying or is likely to carry out any activity which may cause serious prejudice to its customers, or to the soundness, stability and integrity of the financial services sector; (d) in the case of an insurer, has failed, is failing or is likely to fail to maintain a financially sound position in accordance with Part III; (e) has failed to provide any information or document requested by the Commission, he may make an investigation into the business or any part of the business of the licensee or registered person.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for data access and investigations

sections.heading: Suspension of licence
sections.title: Suspension of insurance licenses
sections.summary: The Chief Executive may suspend a licensee's insurance license if they have grounds under section 96 to investigate or if there are grounds for revocation under section 112 or appointment of a conservator under section 106. Suspension requires prior notice to the licensee with opportunity to make representations, except when delay would not be in the public interest. Suspended licensees must cease business activities but remain subject to the Act's provisions. Suspension also affects insurance agents and salespersons.
sections.dtia_summary: Suspension of licenses impacts data transfer and storage by restricting business activities during investigations, requiring data to remain accessible for regulatory purposes.

requirements.text: Chief Executive may suspend wholly or in part a licensee's insurance license
Chief Executive must give prior notice to licensee with reasons for suspension
Licensee must cease business activities related to suspended license
Suspended licensees remain subject to Act provisions
Suspension affects insurance agents and salespersons of the insurer
Commission must give public notice of license suspension decisions

sections.source_quote: Without prejudice to the powers of the Chief Executive under the Financial Services Act 2007, where, at any time it appears to the Chief Executive that – (a) he has grounds under section 96 to carry out an investigation into the business of a licensee; or (b) there are grounds under section 112 for the revocation of a licence or under section 106 for the appointment of a conservator, he may, by notice in writing, suspend wholly or in part, the licence of a licensee.

sections.heading: Effect of suspension of licence
sections.title: Continuation of insurance policies during license suspension
sections.summary: A license suspension does not prevent a licensee from continuing business for insurance policies issued before suspension notification or dealing with policies in pursuance of existing terms. The licensee is deemed licensed for these activities, allowing continued processing of data related to these policies.
sections.dtia_summary: License suspension allows continued data processing for pre-suspension policies, impacting data transfer and storage during regulatory actions.

Licensee may issue or deal with insurance policies in pursuance of existing terms
Licensee is deemed licensed for these activities

sections.relevance_reason: license suspension affects data processing continuity

sections.heading: Power to enter premises
sections.title: Power to enter premises for inspections
sections.summary: Inspectors or investigators may enter a licensee's premises during normal business hours to inspect, copy, or take extracts from documents, books, or records, and access or take possession of computers, discs, cassettes, or microfilm. Obstructing an inspector or investigator is an offence punishable by fines up to 500,000 rupees and imprisonment up to 2 years.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulators can access data stored on physical and digital devices, impacting data transfer and storage across borders through physical and digital data access.

Inspector may take extracts from any documents

sections.heading: Tampering with evidence
sections.title: Tampering with evidence during investigations
sections.summary: Tampering with evidence, including destruction, falsification, concealment, or disposal of documents or information stored on computers or devices relevant to inspections or investigations, is an offence punishable by fines up to 1 million rupees and imprisonment up to 3 years.

sections.source_quote: Any person who – (a) destroys, falsifies, conceals or disposes of; or (b) causes or permits the destruction, falsification, concealment or disposal of, any document, information stored on a computer or other device or other thing, which the person knows, or ought reasonably to have known, to be relevant to an inspection or investigation under this Part shall commit an offence and shall on conviction be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 3 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Tampering with evidence directly impacts data integrity and investigations

sections.heading: Meaning of certain words
sections.title: Definitions for regulatory terms
sections.summary: The Act defines 'licensee' to include registered persons acting on behalf of licensees, past/present officers, employees, associated parties, and corporations with expired or cancelled licenses. 'Chief Executive' includes designated staff or appointed persons. 'Associated party' includes partners, controllers, directors, officers, and related companies.
sections.dtia_summary: Definitions clarify who is subject to regulatory requirements, impacting data transfer and storage obligations for associated parties.

requirements.text: Licensee includes registered persons acting on behalf of licensee
Licensee includes past or present officers, partners, managers, employees, and controllers
Licensee includes corporations with expired, cancelled, suspended, or surrendered licenses
Licensee includes persons carrying out insurance activities without appropriate license
Chief Executive includes designated staff or appointed persons
Associated party includes partners, controllers, directors, officers, and related companies

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of this Part – (a) any reference to a "licensee" shall, save and except for sections 97 and 98, include – (i) a registered person who acts or has acted on behalf of that licensee or any past or present employee of that registered person; (ii) any person who is a past or present officer, partner, manager, employee or controller of that licensee; (iii) an associated party of that licensee; (iv) a corporation whose licence has expired, or has been cancelled, revoked, suspended or surrendered; (v) a person who has carried out or is carrying out any activity in the insurance sector without the appropriate licence under this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines key terms for data compliance

sections.heading: Duties and powers of administrator
sections.title: Administrator powers under Financial Services Act
sections.summary: An administrator appointed by the Commission under the Financial Services Act 2007 must exercise the powers and duties of an administrator as defined in that Act and carry out the activities of a licensee per section 98 of the same Act. This section establishes the foundational responsibilities of the administrator without imposing additional obligations beyond those already specified in the Financial Services Act.

requirements.text: Exercise powers and duties of an administrator under the Financial Services Act 2007
Carry out activities of a licensee in accordance with section 98

sections.source_quote: Where an administrator is appointed by the Commission pursuant to its powers under the Financial Services Act 2007, he shall – (a) have the powers, and shall carry out the duties, of an administrator under the Financial Services Act 2007; and (b) carry out the activities of a licensee in accordance with section 98.
sections.relevance_reason: Governance and record-keeping for financial data

sections.heading: Appointment of conservator
sections.title: Conditions for appointing a conservator
sections.summary: The Commission may appoint a conservator if it reasonably believes the insurer's capital is impaired, the insurer has engaged in practices detrimental to policyholders and creditors, or the insurer's solvency margin is deficient. This section outlines specific scenarios where the Commission can intervene to protect policyholders and creditors by appointing a conservator.

requirements.text: The Commission has reasonable cause to believe the insurer's stated capital is impaired or there is a threat of such impairment
The insurer has engaged in practices detrimental to policyholders and creditors
The insurer or its directors have knowingly or negligently permitted employees to contravene anti-money laundering or terrorism prevention laws
The insurer's assets are not maintained in accordance with solvency rules
The insurer's solvency margin is or is likely to be deficient

sections.source_quote: Where the Commission has reasonable cause to believe that - (a) the stated capital of the insurer is impaired or there is a threat of such impairment; or (b) the insurer has, or its directors have - (i) engaged in practices detrimental to the interests of its policyholders and creditors; (ii) knowingly or negligently permitted its chief executive officer, any of its other managers, officers, employees, insurance agents or salesperson to contravene any provision of this Act, or any FSC Rule, or any enactment relating to anti-money laundering or prevention of terrorism or guidelines and directions issued by the Commission; (c) contraventions referred to in paragraph (b)(ii) are likely to occur; (d) the assets of the insurer are not being maintained in accordance with solvency rules, or is not sufficient to give adequate protection to its policyholders or creditors; (e) the solvency margin of the insurer is or is likely to be deficient
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for financial data protection

sections.heading: Duties and powers of conservator
sections.title: Conservator's powers and duties
sections.summary: The conservator takes charge of the insurer's business and assets, may overrule the board of directors, suspend director powers, manage claims, enforce contracts, and maintain separate accounts for funds received during conservatorship. The conservator must report to the Commission if not an employee of the Commission.

requirements.text: Take charge of the business of the insurer and all of its property, books, records and effects
Exercise powers necessary to preserve, protect and recover assets of the insurer
Collect all monies and debts due to the insurer
Assert causes of action belonging to the insurer and file, sue and defend suits on its behalf
Overrule or revoke actions of the board of directors and management
Suspend the powers of the board of directors during conservatorship
Suspend repayment or withdrawal of claims and other liabilities of the insurer
Disaffirm or repudiate any contract to which the insurer is a party
Enforce any contract entered into by the insurer, notwithstanding termination clauses
Disaffirm or repudiate a contract that, in his opinion, is fraudulent
Keep money and credits received in a separate account and not use them to liquidate existing or subsequent indebtedness
Report to and be accountable to the Commission if not an employee of the Commission

sections.source_quote: The conservator shall take charge of the business of the insurer and all of its property, books, records and effects and shall exercise all powers necessary to preserve, protect and recover any of the assets of the insurer, collect all monies and debts due to it, assert causes of action belonging to the insurer and file, sue and defend suits on its behalf.

sections.heading: Term of office and remuneration of conservator

sections.summary: The conservator's term continues until the Commission finds the insurer is rehabilitated or reorganized, or the insurer's continuation would not cause probable loss to policyholders and creditors. The insurer bears the conservator's remuneration and indemnification costs, and directors must resume their roles unless deemed unfit by the Commission.

requirements.text: The conservator's term continues until the Commission finds the insurer is rehabilitated or reorganized
The conservator's term continues until the Commission finds the insurer's continuation would not involve probable loss to policyholders and creditors

Directors resume their offices unless found by the Commission unfit to resume directorship

sections.source_quote: The term of office of the conservator shall continue, unless replaced by a successor, until such time as the Commission finds that - (a) the insurer is rehabilitated or reorganised, so that it may be returned to management or a new management under such conditions as are necessary to prevent recurrence of the conditions that gave rise to the conservatorship; or (b) the insurer is in such condition that its continuance in business would not involve probable loss to its policyholders and other creditors.

sections.heading: Rehabilitation or reorganisation of insurer
sections.title: Rehabilitation or reorganization of insurer
sections.summary: The conservator seeks Commission authority to rehabilitate the insurer or reorganize it, proposing a reorganization plan after hearing interested parties. The Commission approves the plan if it is equitable, provides new funds to meet solvency margins, and removes officers responsible for the conservatorship conditions.

sections.heading: Immunity of the administrator, conservator and special

sections.heading: Surrender of licence

sections.heading: Revocation of licence
sections.title: Conditions for revoking insurance licence
sections.summary: The Chief Executive may refer a licensee to the Enforcement Committee for revocation if the licensee has ceased business, failed to commence within 6 months, failed to meet solvency, contravened regulations, or engaged in misconduct. The Enforcement Committee follows procedures from section 53 of the Financial Services Act 2007. Revocation allows public notice and directions to protect policyholders.

requirements.text: The Chief Executive may refer to Enforcement Committee if licensee has ceased business for which licensed

The Chief Executive may refer to Enforcement Committee if licensee contravenes regulations
The Chief Executive may refer to Enforcement Committee if licensee provides false information

sections.source_quote: Where it appears to the Chief Executive that - (a) the licensee has ceased the business for which it was licensed; (b) the licensee has failed to commence business within 6 months from the date on which it was licensed; (c) there exists a ground which, under any provision of this Act and FSC Rules would have prevented the licensee from being licensed; (d) the licensee has failed to satisfy an obligation to which it is subject by virtue of this Act and FSC Rules; (e) the licensee is unable to meet its financial obligation or the solvency margin; (f) the business of the license is not being carried on in accordance with sound insurance principles; (g) the licensee is contravening or has contravened any of the provisions of this Act or any conditions imposed on its licence or any directions given by the Commission under this Act, regardless that there has been no conviction for an offence in respect of such contravention; (h) the licensee, or any of its officers responsible for its management has furnished false or misleading information or has concealed, or failed to disclose material facts in its application for a licence or in any returns filed under this Act or FSC Rules; (i) the licensee, or any of its directors or officers responsible for its management has been convicted of an offence under this Act or an offence relating to a financial crime under any other enactment whether in Mauritius or elsewhere; (j) the licensee proposes to make or has made a composition or arrangement with its creditors or goes into receivership or liquidation, is wound up or is dissolved; or (k) an insurer, being a foreign company, has ceased to be authorised to issue insurance policies, or contracts of a particular description, in the country where it has its head office, the Chief Executive shall forthwith refer the matter to the Enforcement Committee to determine whether the license should be revoked.

sections.title: FSC Rules and guidelines for insurance
sections.summary: The Commission may create FSC Rules and guidelines for insurance, including consultation processes, publication of proposed rules, and exemptions from certain rules. These rules must be consistent with the Act and may exempt persons from rule application.
sections.dtia_summary: FSC Rules and guidelines provide mechanisms for regulatory oversight of insurance data handling, including consultation requirements and exemption provisions that impact data transfer DTIAs.

requirements.text: Commission may make FSC Rules not inconsistent with regulations
Commission may issue guidelines for the purposes of this Act
Commission must publish proposed FSC Rules for consultation
Commission must state where proposed FSC Rules can be accessed
Commission may publish FSC Rules in the Gazette
Commission may exempt persons from FSC Rule application

sections.source_quote: The Commission may, for the purposes of this Act, make rules to be known as the FSC Rules not inconsistent with any regulations to give effect to this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for financial data rules

sections.heading: Transitional Provisions
sections.title: Transitional provisions for insurance
sections.summary: This section defines transitional terms for existing insurance entities, including insurers, intermediaries, and managers, and specifies that the Act applies to these entities while preserving certain tax exemptions and winding-up proceedings. It also clarifies that Category 1 Global Business Licences granted under the Financial Services Act 2007 remain valid.
sections.dtia_summary: Transitional provisions ensure continuity of data handling for existing entities, with specific exemptions for tax purposes and winding-up proceedings that may affect data transfer DTIAs.

requirements.text: Category 1 Global Business Licence has same meaning as in Financial Services Act 2007
Existing insurance intermediary includes agents, brokers, and salesmen
Existing insurance manager includes captive management companies
Existing insurer includes registered insurers and reinsurers
Act applies to existing insurers, intermediaries, and managers
Category 1 Global Business Licences granted under Financial Services Act 2.007 remain valid
Existing insurers may be exempt from tax if they transfer assets to separate business
Winding-up proceedings for existing entities continue under repealed Act

sections.source_quote: In this section – 'Category 1 Global Business Licence' has the same meaning as in the Financial Services Act 2007; 'existing insurance intermediary' means an insurance agent, an insurance broker and an insurance salesman registered under the repealed Act; 'existing insurance manager' means a captive management company holding a Category 1 Global Business Licence issued under the Financial Services Act 2007 immediately before the coming into operation of this Act; 'existing insurer' means an insurer or reinsurer registered under the repealed Act, and includes an insurer or reinsurer holding a Category 1 Global Business Licence issued immediately before the coming into operation of this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: tax exemptions, winding-up, licenses

sections.heading: Consequential amendments
sections.title: Consequential amendments to other laws
sections.summary: This section amends multiple laws to incorporate insurance regulations, including the Code Civil Mauricien, Non-Citizens (Property Restriction) Act, Protected Cell Companies Act, and Road Traffic Act. These amendments define business certificates, qualified corporations, and cell company structures for insurance activities.
sections.dtia_summary: Consequential amendments establish legal frameworks for insurance data handling across multiple laws, impacting data transfer DTIAs through regulatory definitions and requirements.

requirements.text: Code Civil Mauricien amended to include insurance regulations
Non-Citizens (Property Restriction) Act amended to define business certificates
Protected Cell Companies Act amended to define qualified corporations
Road Traffic Act amended to specify insurance requirements

sections.source_quote: This section amends the Code Civil Mauricien, Non-Citizens (Property Restriction) Act, Protected Cell Companies Act, and Road Traffic Act to incorporate insurance regulations.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: defines structures for insurance activities

sections.heading: Commencement
sections.title: Commencement of insurance Act
sections.summary: This section specifies the effective date of the Act, allowing different sections to come into operation on different dates. The Act was passed by the National Assembly on March 25, 2005.
sections.dtia_summary: Commencement provisions determine when the Act takes effect, which impacts the timeline for data transfer DTIAs and regulatory compliance.

requirements.text: Act comes into operation on a date fixed by Proclamation
Different sections may have different effective dates

sections.source_quote: Subject to subsection (2), this Act shall come into operation on a date to be fixed by Proclamation.
sections.relevance_reason: commencement date irrelevant to compliance

sections.title: Existing insurers deemed licensed under new act
sections.summary: Existing insurers retain their pre-2005 licenses for specific insurance categories until the new act's effective date. This includes long-term and general insurance businesses, significant shareholders, and solvency margins. The Commission may intervene if insurers fail to meet solvency requirements during the transition period.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes transitional licensing for insurers, ensuring continuity of operations during the act's implementation. It affects data transfer by maintaining existing regulatory frameworks for insurance data handling.

requirements.text: An existing insurer shall be deemed to hold a licence under this Act for any category of insurance business it was registered under the repealed Act
An existing insurer carrying both long-term and general insurance business shall be deemed authorised under section 8 until the effective date
Significant shareholders of existing insurers are deemed to have obtained Commission authorisation under section 27
Stated capital and deposit requirements under the repealed Act are deemed to be those required by this Act for existing insurers
Section 26(6) of the repealed Act remains in force for existing insurers until the effective date
Existing insurers must satisfy the Commission with an annual contingency plan ensuring solvency compliance by the effective date
The Commission may take appropriate actions under the Act during the contingency plan period

sections.source_quote: An existing insurer shall, until the effective date, be deemed to hold a licence issued under this Act in respect of any category or class of insurance business for which it was registered under the repealed Act or licensed under the repealed Financial Services Development Act 2001 immediately before the coming into operation of this Act or unless the licence is earlier cancelled or revoked under Part XII.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for solvency compliance during transition

sections.heading: Existing insurance intermediaries and manager
sections.title: Existing insurance intermediaries deemed licensed
sections.summary: Existing insurance intermediaries and managers are deemed licensed or registered under the new act until January 1, 2008, unless their license or registration is revoked earlier. This ensures continuity of intermediaries' regulatory status during the transition period.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures existing insurance intermediaries maintain regulatory status during transition, which impacts data handling and transfer processes for intermediary data.

requirements.text: An existing insurance intermediary or manager shall be deemed licensed or registered under this Act as an insurance manager, agent, broker or salesperson

sections.source_quote: An existing insurance intermediary or manager shall, until 1 January 2008, be deemed to be licensed or registered under this Act as an insurance manager, agent, broker or salesperson, as the case may be unless the licence or registration is earlier cancelled or revoked under this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Deemed licensing affects financial data handling

sections.heading: Existing registers
sections.title: Existing registers deemed established under new act
sections.summary: Registers maintained by the Commission for existing insurers, managers and intermediaries are deemed to be established and maintained under section 6 of the new act. This provides continuity for regulatory records during the transition period.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures regulatory records for existing entities remain valid under the new act, which affects data storage and transfer compliance for insurance-related data.

requirements.text: Any register held by the Commission relating to existing insurers, insurance managers and intermediaries shall be deemed established and maintained under section 6 of this Act

sections.source_quote: Any register held by the Commission relating to existing insurers, insurance managers and intermediaries shall be deemed to have been established and maintained under section 6 of this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory continuity for financial data registers
